([錢唐](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E9%8C%A2%E5%94%90 "wikt:錢唐")) as a county under the direction of [Kuaiji Commandery](/wiki/Kuaiji_Commandery "Kuaiji Commandery") (now [Shaoxing](/wiki/Shaoxing "Shaoxing")). It was located in the area of the Wulin Mountains and the Wulin Lakes. Under the Han, the same area was known as **Wulin** ([武林](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E6%AD%A6%E6%9E%97 "wikt:武林")).[[22]](#cite_note-23)

**Hangzhou**[[a]](#cite_note-9) is the [capital city](/wiki/Capital_city "Capital city") and most populous city of [Zhejiang](/wiki/Zhejiang "Zhejiang") province, [China](/wiki/China "China"), situated at the intersection of the southern terminus of the [Grand Canal](/wiki/Grand_Canal_(China) "Grand Canal (China)") and the head of [Hangzhou Bay](/wiki/Hangzhou_Bay "Hangzhou Bay").[[9]](#cite_note-:04-10): 86 Renowned as one of the [Eight Ancient Capitals of China](/wiki/Historical_capitals_of_China "Historical capitals of China"), Hangzhou is celebrated for its cultural heritage and its [West Lake](/wiki/West_Lake "West Lake"), a [UNESCO World Heritage site](/wiki/UNESCO_World_Heritage_site "UNESCO World Heritage site").

Established as a [county seat](/wiki/County_seat "County seat") in 221 [BC](/wiki/BCE "BCE"), Hangzhou later served as the capital of the [Wuyue Kingdom](/wiki/Wuyue "Wuyue") (923–997) and the [Southern Song dynasty](/wiki/Southern_Song_dynasty "Southern Song dynasty") (1138–1276).[[10]](#cite_note-11) The city has three [UNESCO World Heritage Sites](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site"), which are the [West Lake Cultural Landscape](/wiki/West_Lake "West Lake"), the [Grand Canal](/wiki/Grand_Canal_(China) "Grand Canal (China)"), and the [Archaeological Ruins of Liangzhu City](/wiki/Archaeological_ruins_of_Liangzhu_City "Archaeological ruins of Liangzhu City").[[11]](#cite_note-12)

Hangzhou is designated as a [sub-provincial city](/wiki/Sub-provincial_city "Sub-provincial city").[[12]](#cite_note-docin1-13) Hangzhou ranked ninth in GDP among mainland Chinese cities and 14th according to the [Global Innovation Index](/wiki/Global_Innovation_Index "Global Innovation Index").[[13]](#cite_note-14)[[14]](#cite_note-15) The city hosts the headquarters of [Alibaba Group](/wiki/Alibaba_Group "Alibaba Group"), [Ant Group](/wiki/Ant_Group "Ant Group"), [DeepSeek](/wiki/DeepSeek "DeepSeek"), [Geely](/wiki/Geely "Geely"), and [NetEase](/wiki/NetEase "NetEase").[[15]](#cite_note-16) According to the [Nature Index](/wiki/Nature_Index "Nature Index"), it ranks 10th globally in scientific research output.[[16]](#cite_note-17)[[17]](#cite_note-18)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hangzhou&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

See also: [Timeline of Hangzhou](/wiki/Timeline_of_Hangzhou "Timeline of Hangzhou")

### Early history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hangzhou&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early history")]

The celebrated [neolithic](/wiki/Chinese_Neolithic "Chinese Neolithic") culture of [Hemudu](/wiki/Hemudu_culture "Hemudu culture") is known to have inhabited [Yuyao](/wiki/Yuyao "Yuyao"), 100 km (62 mi) south-east of Hangzhou as far back as seven thousand years ago.[[18]](#cite_note-19) It was during this time that rice was first cultivated in southeast China.[[19]](#cite_note-20) Excavations have established that the [jade](/wiki/Chinese_jade "Chinese jade")-carving [Liangzhu culture](/wiki/Liangzhu_culture "Liangzhu culture") (named for its [type site](/wiki/Type_site "Type site") just northwest of Hangzhou) inhabited the area immediately around the present city around five thousand years ago.[[20]](#cite_note-21) The first of Hangzhou's present neighborhoods to appear in written records was [Yuhang](/wiki/Yuhang "Yuhang"), which probably preserves an old [Baiyue](/wiki/Baiyue "Baiyue") name.[[21]](#cite_note-22)

In 222 BC, the [First Emperor](/wiki/First_Emperor_of_Qin "First Emperor of Qin") of the [Qin](/wiki/Qin_Dynasty "Qin Dynasty") established **Qiantang** ([錢唐](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E9%8C%A2%E5%94%90 "wikt:錢唐")) as a county under the direction of [Kuaiji Commandery](/wiki/Kuaiji_Commandery "Kuaiji Commandery") (now [Shaoxing](/wiki/Shaoxing "Shaoxing")). It was located in the area of the Wulin Mountains and the Wulin Lakes. Under the Han, the same area was known as **Wulin** ([武林](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E6%AD%A6%E6%9E%97 "wikt:武林")).[[22]](#cite_note-23)

Hangzhou was made the seat of the [prefecture](/wiki/Zhou_(Chinese_province) "Zhou (Chinese province)") of [Hang](/wiki/Hang_Prefecture "Hang Prefecture") under the [Sui](/wiki/Sui_dynasty "Sui dynasty") in AD 589, entitling it to a [city wall](/wiki/Chinese_city_wall "Chinese city wall") which was constructed two years later. By a longstanding convention also seen in other cities like [Guangzhou](/wiki/Guangzhou "Guangzhou") and [Fuzhou](/wiki/Fuzhou "Fuzhou"), the city took on the name of the area it administered and became known as Hangzhou. Hangzhou was at the southern end of China's [Grand Canal](/wiki/Grand_Canal_(China) "Grand Canal (China)") which extends to Beijing. The canal evolved over centuries but reached its full length by 609.[[23]](#cite_note-ebrey_cambridge_114-24)

### Tang dynasty

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hangzhou&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Tang dynasty")]

In the [Tang dynasty](/wiki/Tang_dynasty "Tang dynasty"), [Bai Juyi](/wiki/Bai_Juyi "Bai Juyi"), a renowned poet, was appointed governor of Hangzhou.[[24]](#cite_note-Waley_1941,_131-25) He noticed that the farmland nearby depended on the water of [West Lake](/wiki/West_Lake "West Lake"), but due to negligence the old [dyke](/wiki/Dike_(construction) "Dike (construction)") had collapsed, and the lake so dried out that the local farmers were suffering from severe [drought](/wiki/Drought "Drought"). He ordered the construction of a stronger and taller dyke, with a [dam](/wiki/Dam "Dam") to control the flow of water, mitigating the drought problem. The livelihood of local people of Hangzhou improved over the following years. Bai Juyi used his leisure time to enjoy the West Lake, visiting it almost daily. He then had willows and other trees planted along the dyke, making it a landmark.

It is listed as one of the [Seven Ancient Capitals of China](/wiki/Historical_capitals_of_China "Historical capitals of China"). It was first the capital of the [Wuyue Kingdom](/wiki/Wuyue_Kingdom "Wuyue Kingdom") from 907 to 978[[9]](#cite_note-:04-10): 86 during the [Five Dynasties and Ten Kingdoms period](/wiki/Five_Dynasties_and_Ten_Kingdoms_period "Five Dynasties and Ten Kingdoms period"). Named **Xifu** ([西府](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E8%A5%BF%E5%BA%9C "wikt:西府")) at the time,[[25]](#cite_note-26) it was one of the three great bastions of culture in [southern China](/wiki/Northern_and_southern_China "Northern and southern China") during the tenth century, along with [Nanjing](/wiki/Nanjing "Nanjing") and [Chengdu](/wiki/Chengdu "Chengdu").[[26]](#cite_note-Worthy_1983_19-27) Leaders of Wuyue were noted patrons of the arts, particularly of [Buddhist](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") temple architecture and artwork. The dyke built to protect the city by King [Qian Liu](/wiki/Qian_Liu "Qian Liu") gave the [Qiantang](/wiki/Qiantang_River "Qiantang River") its modern name.[[27]](#cite_note-28) Hangzhou also became a cosmopolitan center, drawing scholars from throughout China and conducting diplomacy with neighboring Chinese states, and also with [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan"), [Goryeo](/wiki/Goryeo "Goryeo"), and the [Khitan](/wiki/Khitan_people "Khitan people") [Liao dynasty](/wiki/Liao_dynasty "Liao dynasty").

### Song dynasty

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hangzhou&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Song dynasty")]

In 1089, another renowned poet governor [Su Shi](/wiki/Su_Shi "Su Shi") (Su Dongpo) used 200,000 workers to construct a 2.8 km (1.7 mi) long [causeway](/wiki/Causeway "Causeway") across West Lake made of mud dredged from the lake bottom. The lake is surrounded by hills on the northern and western sides. The [Baochu Pagoda](/wiki/Baochu_Pagoda "Baochu Pagoda") sits on the Baoshi Hill to the north of the lake.

Hangzhou was chosen as the new capital of the [Southern Song dynasty](/wiki/Southern_Song_dynasty "Southern Song dynasty") in 1132,[[28]](#cite_note-29) when most of [northern China](/wiki/Northern_China "Northern China") had been conquered by the Jurchens in the [Jin–Song wars](/wiki/Jin%E2%80%93Song_wars "Jin–Song wars").[[29]](#cite_note-30) The surviving [imperial family](/wiki/House_of_Zhao "House of Zhao") had retreated south from its original capital in [Kaifeng](/wiki/Kaifeng "Kaifeng") after it was captured by the [Jurchens](/wiki/Jurchen_people "Jurchen people") in the [Jingkang Incident](/wiki/Jingkang_Incident "Jingkang Incident") of 1127.[[30]](#cite_note-moteb-31)[[31]](#cite_note-32) [Emperor Gaozong](/wiki/Emperor_Gaozong_of_Song "Emperor Gaozong of Song") moved to Nanjing, then to modern [Shangqiu](/wiki/Shangqiu "Shangqiu"), then to [Yangzhou](/wiki/Yangzhou "Yangzhou") in 1128, and finally to Hangzhou in 1129.[[30]](#cite_note-moteb-31)[[32]](#cite_note-mote-33)

Once the prospect of retaking northern China had diminished, buildings in Hangzhou were extended and renovated to become a permanent imperial capital. The imperial palace in Hangzhou, modest in size, was expanded in 1133 with new roofed alleyways, and in 1148 with an extension of the palace walls.[[33]](#cite_note-34) The city walls were built with [tamped earth](/wiki/Tamped_earth "Tamped earth") and stone and was 30 feet high and 10 feet thick at its base. There were 13 gates and several towers on the walls. The walls covered the city by four miles north to south and only one mile east to west.[[34]](#cite_note-:0-35) According to the Italian explorer [Odoric of Pordenone](/wiki/Odoric_of_Pordenone "Odoric of Pordenone"), Hangzhou was the greatest city in the world. It was heavily populated and filled with large family estates. It had 12,000 bridges. Bread, pork, rice, and wine were abundant despite the large population.[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYule2002128-36) Arab merchants lived in Hangzhou during the [Song dynasty](/wiki/Song_dynasty "Song dynasty"), due to the fact that the oceangoing trade passages took precedence over land trade during this time.[[36]](#cite_note-37) The [Phoenix Mosque](/wiki/Phoenix_Mosque "Phoenix Mosque") was constructed by a Persian settler in Hangzhou at this time.[[37]](#cite_note-38)

From 1132 until the [Mongol](/wiki/Mongol_Empire "Mongol Empire") invasion of 1276, Hangzhou remained the capital of the Southern Song dynasty and was known as **Lin'an** ([臨安](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E8%87%A8%E5%AE%89 "wikt:臨安")). It served as the seat of the imperial government, a center of trade and entertainment, and the nexus of the main branches of the [civil service](/wiki/Civil_service "Civil service"). During that time the city was a gravitational center of Chinese civilization as what used to be considered "central China" in the north was taken by the [Jin](/wiki/Jin_dynasty_(1115%E2%80%931234) "Jin dynasty (1115–1234)"), an ethnic minority dynasty ruled by [Jurchens](/wiki/Jurchen_people "Jurchen people").

Numerous philosophers, politicians, and men of literature, including some of the most celebrated poets in Chinese history such as [Su Shi](/wiki/Su_Shi "Su Shi"), [Lu You](/wiki/Lu_You "Lu You"), and [Xin Qiji](/wiki/Xin_Qiji "Xin Qiji") came here to live and die. Hangzhou is also the birthplace and final resting place of the scientist [Shen Kuo](/wiki/Shen_Kuo "Shen Kuo") (1031–1095 AD), his tomb being located in the [Yuhang](/wiki/Yuhang "Yuhang") district.[[38]](#cite_note-Yuhang-39)

During the Southern Song dynasty, commercial expansion, an influx of refugees from the conquered north, and the growth of the official and military establishments, led to a corresponding population increase and the city developed well outside its 9th-century ramparts. According to the *[Encyclopædia Britannica](/wiki/Encyclop%C3%A6dia_Britannica "Encyclopædia Britannica")*, Hangzhou had a population of over 2 million at that time, while historian [Jacques Gernet](/wiki/Jacques_Gernet "Jacques Gernet") has estimated that the population of Hangzhou numbered well over one million by 1276. (Official Chinese census figures from the year 1270 listed some 186,330 families in residence and probably failed to count non-residents and soldiers.) It is believed that Hangzhou was the largest city in the world from 1180 to 1315 and from 1348 to 1358.[[39]](#cite_note-40)[[40]](#cite_note-41)

Because of the large population and densely crowded (often multi-story) wooden buildings, Hangzhou was particularly vulnerable to fires. Major conflagrations destroyed large sections of the city in 1208, 1229, 1237, and 1275. The 1237 fire alone destroyed 30,000 dwellings. However, the worst was the 1208 fire which burned for 4 days in a 3-mile diameter and burnt 58,097 houses as well as killing 59 people. To combat this threat, the city constructed storage buildings that were rented out to merchants where watchmen patrolled by night and was enclosed by water on all sides.[[34]](#cite_note-:0-35) Besides this, the government established an elaborate system for fighting fires, erected watchtowers, devised a system of lantern and flag signals to identify the source of the flames and direct the response, and charged more than 3,000 soldiers with the task of putting out fire.

### Yuan dynasty

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hangzhou&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Yuan dynasty")]

Hangzhou was besieged and captured by the advancing [Mongol](/wiki/Mongol "Mongol") armies of [Kublai Khan](/wiki/Kublai_Khan "Kublai Khan") in 1276, three years before the final collapse of the Southern Song.[[41]](#cite_note-gernet_15-42) Historian [Patricia Buckley Ebrey](/wiki/Patricia_Buckley_Ebrey "Patricia Buckley Ebrey") noted that the Mongol [Yuan dynasty](/wiki/Yuan_dynasty "Yuan dynasty") killed the Jurchen Wanyan royal family by the hundreds in the [Siege of Kaifeng (1232)](/wiki/Siege_of_Kaifeng_(1232) "Siege of Kaifeng (1232)"), while sparing the city of Hangzhou including the Chinese Zhao royal family of the [Southern Song](/wiki/Song_dynasty "Song dynasty"). The Mongols rehired Southern Song government officials and had Han Chinese artisans in [Shangdu](/wiki/Shangdu "Shangdu") marry the palace women.[[42]](#cite_note-43) The capital of the new [Yuan dynasty](/wiki/Yuan_dynasty "Yuan dynasty") was established in the city of [Dadu](/wiki/Khanbaliq "Khanbaliq") ([Beijing](/wiki/Beijing "Beijing")), but Hangzhou remained an important commercial and administrative center for their southern territory.

#### Foreign descriptions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hangzhou&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Foreign descriptions")]

Yuan China was very open to foreign visitors, and several returned west describing Hangzhou—under the names **Khinzai**,[[43]](#cite_note-44) **Campsay**,[[44]](#cite_note-45) etc.[[note 1]](#cite_note-48)—as one of the foremost cities in the world. The [Venetian](/wiki/Republic_of_Venice "Republic of Venice") merchant [Marco Polo](/wiki/Marco_Polo "Marco Polo") supposedly visited Hangzhou in the late 13th century. In [his book](/wiki/The_Travels_of_Marco_Polo "The Travels of Marco Polo"), he records that the city was "greater than any in the world"[[32]](#cite_note-mote-33) and that "the number and wealth of the merchants, and the amount of goods that passed through their hands, was so enormous that no man could form a just estimate thereof". Polo's account greatly exaggerates the city's size, although it has been argued that the "hundred miles" of walls would be plausible if [Chinese miles](/wiki/Li_(unit) "Li (unit)") were intended instead of [Italian ones](/wiki/Italian_mile "Italian mile")[[47]](#cite_note-49) and that the "12,000 stone bridges" might have been a copyist error born from the city's 12 gates.[[48]](#cite_note-50) In the 14th century, the [Moroccan](/wiki/Morocco "Morocco") traveler [Ibn Battuta](/wiki/Ibn_Battuta "Ibn Battuta") arrived; his later account concurred that **al-Khansā** was "the biggest city I have ever seen on the face of the earth."[[49]](#cite_note-51)[[50]](#cite_note-52)[[51]](#cite_note-Ibn-53) He visited Hangzhou in 1345 and noted its charm and said the city was on [West Lake](/wiki/West_Lake "West Lake") and surrounded by gentle green hills.[[52]](#cite_note-54) He said there were a large number of well-crafted and well-painted Chinese wooden ships with colored sails and silk awnings in the canals. He attended a banquet held by Qurtai, the [Yuan](/wiki/Yuan_dynasty "Yuan dynasty") Mongol administrator of the city, who according to Ibn Battuta, was fond of the skills of local Chinese [conjurers](/wiki/Evocation "Evocation").[[53]](#cite_note-55) Hangzhou's reputation among Europeans of the time was such that it was the intended destination of [Christopher Columbus](/wiki/Christopher_Columbus "Christopher Columbus")' [first voyage](/wiki/First_Voyage_of_Columbus "First Voyage of Columbus") to the Americas in 1492-93.[[54]](#cite_note-56)

### Modern history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hangzhou&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Modern history")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f7/Hangzhou_CBD_%28Cropped%29.jpg/250px-Hangzhou_CBD_%28Cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hangzhou_CBD_(Cropped).jpg)

Hangzhou CBD

The city remained an important port until the middle of the [Ming dynasty](/wiki/Ming_dynasty "Ming dynasty") era, when its harbor slowly [silted](/wiki/Silt "Silt") up. Under the [Qing](/wiki/Qing_dynasty "Qing dynasty"), it was the site of an [imperial army](/wiki/Qing_Army "Qing Army") garrison.[[55]](#cite_note-57)

In 1856 and 1860, the [Taiping Heavenly Kingdom](/wiki/Taiping_Heavenly_Kingdom "Taiping Heavenly Kingdom") occupied Hangzhou. The city was heavily damaged during its conquest, occupation, and eventual reconquest by the Qing army.

After the collapse of the Qing dynasty, control of Hangzhou was [contested by warlords](/wiki/Warlord_Era "Warlord Era"), particularly those of the [Anhui](/wiki/Anhui_clique "Anhui clique") and [Zhili cliques](/wiki/Zhili_clique "Zhili clique"), until the [Kuomintang](/wiki/Kuomintang "Kuomintang")'s successful [Northern Expedition](/wiki/Northern_Expedition "Northern Expedition"). It was then more fully administered by the [Republic of China](/wiki/Republic_of_China_(1912%E2%80%931949) "Republic of China (1912–1949)") from 1927 to 1937. From 1937 to 1945, the city was occupied by [Japan](/wiki/Second_Sino-Japanese_War "Second Sino-Japanese War"). The Kuomintang returned in 1945 and governed until May 3, 1949, when the [People's Liberation Army](/wiki/People%27s_Liberation_Army "People's Liberation Army") entered Hangzhou and the city came under [Chinese Communist Party](/wiki/Chinese_Communist_Party "Chinese Communist Party") (CCP) control. During the late [Cultural Revolution](/wiki/Cultural_Revolution "Cultural Revolution"), Hangzhou was stage to a series of labor unrest and factional fighting known as the [1975 Hangzhou incident](/wiki/Hangzhou_incident_(1975) "Hangzhou incident (1975)"). After [Deng Xiaoping](/wiki/Deng_Xiaoping "Deng Xiaoping")'s [reform policies](/wiki/Reform_and_Opening_Up "Reform and Opening Up") started being enacted in 1978, Hangzhou took advantage of being situated in the [Yangtze Delta](/wiki/Yangtze_Delta "Yangtze Delta") to bolster its development. It is now one of China's most prosperous major cities and hosted the [eleventh G20 summit](/wiki/2016_G20_Hangzhou_summit "2016 G20 Hangzhou summit") in 2016.[[56]](#cite_note-58)

In February 2020,[*[until when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] the city undertook strong curfew measures owing to the [coronavirus outbreak](/wiki/2019-20_coronavirus_outbreak "2019-20 coronavirus outbreak") that [spread across China](/wiki/Timeline_of_the_2019%E2%80%9320_coronavirus_outbreak "Timeline of the 2019–20 coronavirus outbreak") from [Wuhan](/wiki/Wuhan "Wuhan").[[57]](#cite_note-59)[[58]](#cite_note-60)

In 2022, Hangzhou [became the third Chinese city to host the Asian Games](/wiki/2022_Asian_Games "2022 Asian Games"), after [Beijing in 1990](/wiki/1990_Asian_Games "1990 Asian Games") and [Guangzhou in 2010](/wiki/2010_Asian_Games "2010 Asian Games").[[59]](#cite_note-61)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hangzhou&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Geography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/Eastchina_and_Eastchina_Sea_tmo_2017313_geo.tif/lossy-page1-250px-Eastchina_and_Eastchina_Sea_tmo_2017313_geo.tif.jpg)](/wiki/File:Eastchina_and_Eastchina_Sea_tmo_2017313_geo.tif)

A satellite image of the [Yangtze River Delta](/wiki/Yangtze_River_Delta "Yangtze River Delta"). The [Yangtze](/wiki/Yangtze "Yangtze")'s natural [sediment discharge](/wiki/Sediment_transport "Sediment transport") can be seen.

Hangzhou is located in northwestern Zhejiang province, at the southern end of the [Grand Canal of China](/wiki/Grand_Canal_of_China "Grand Canal of China"), which runs to [Beijing](/wiki/Beijing "Beijing"), in the south-central portion of the [Yangtze River Delta](/wiki/Yangtze_River_Delta "Yangtze River Delta"). Its administrative area (sub-provincial city) extends west to the mountainous parts of [Anhui](/wiki/Anhui "Anhui") province, and east to the coastal plain near [Hangzhou Bay](/wiki/Hangzhou_Bay "Hangzhou Bay").[[9]](#cite_note-:04-10): 86 The city center is built around the eastern and northern sides of the West Lake, just north of the [Qiantang River](/wiki/Qiantang_River "Qiantang River").[[9]](#cite_note-:04-10): 86

The [Qiantang River](/wiki/Qiantang_River "Qiantang River") is the largest river in Zhejiang Province, China. Every year during August 15 to August 18 of the lunar month in China, the Qiantang Tide occurs. It is called "the Biggest Tide in the World". The world's largest [tidal bore](/wiki/Tidal_bore "Tidal bore") races up the Qiantang River through Hangzhou reaching up to 12 m (39 ft) in height.

Further information: [List of hills in Hangzhou](/wiki/List_of_hills_in_Hangzhou "List of hills in Hangzhou")

Hangzhou's climate is [humid subtropical](/wiki/Humid_subtropical "Humid subtropical") ([Köppen](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") *Cfa*) with four distinct seasons, characterised by long, very hot, humid summers and chilly, cloudy and drier winters, albeit with occasional snow. The mean annual temperature is 17.0 °C (62.6 °F), with monthly daily averages ranging from 5 °C (41.0 °F) in January to 29.3 °C (84.7 °F) in July. The city receives an average annual rainfall of 1,438 mm (56.6 in) and is affected by the [plum rains](/wiki/Meiyu "Meiyu") of the Asian monsoon in June. In late summer (August to September), Hangzhou suffers [typhoon](/wiki/Typhoon "Typhoon") storms, but typhoons seldom strike it directly. Generally they make landfall along the southern coast of Zhejiang, and affect the area with strong winds and stormy rains.[[60]](#cite_note-62) Extremes since 1951 have ranged from −9.6 °C (15 °F) on 6 February 1969 up to 41.9 °C (107 °F) on 3 August 2024;[[61]](#cite_note-63)[[62]](#cite_note-64) unofficial readings have reached −10.5 °C (13 °F), set on 29 December 1912 and 24 January 1916, up to 42.1 °C (108 °F), set on 10 August 1930.[[63]](#cite_note-65) With monthly percent possible sunshine ranging from 30% in March to 51% in August, the city receives 1,709.4 hours of sunshine annually.

As of 2023, Hangzhou had a permanent population of 12.522 million (including Xiaoshan and Yuhang), of which 10.543 million (84.2%) lived in urban areas.[[70]](#cite_note-72)[[71]](#cite_note-73) The encompassing [metropolitan area](/wiki/Metropolitan_area "Metropolitan area") was estimated by the [OECD](/wiki/OECD "OECD") (Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development) to have, as of 2010[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Hangzhou&action=edit), a population of 13.4 million,[[72]](#cite_note-oecd2015-74) although other sources put the figure at over 21 million. The Hangzhou metropolitan area includes the major cities of [Shaoxing](/wiki/Shaoxing "Shaoxing"), [Jiaxing](/wiki/Jiaxing "Jiaxing") and [Huzhou](/wiki/Huzhou "Huzhou").[[73]](#cite_note-ssdph.com.cn-75)[[74]](#cite_note-76)

Hangzhou has a [life expectancy](/wiki/Life_expectancy "Life expectancy") of 83.18 years for the city's registered population as of 2021[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Hangzhou&action=edit), one of the highest in China.[[75]](#cite_note-77)

### Religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hangzhou&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Religion")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **needs additional citations for [verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Hangzhou "Special:EditPage/Hangzhou") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners") in this section. Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *(April 2018)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

In 1848, during the [Qing dynasty](/wiki/Qing_dynasty "Qing dynasty"), Hangzhou was described as the "stronghold" of [Islam in China](/wiki/Islam_in_China "Islam in China"), the city containing several mosques with Arabic inscriptions.[[76]](#cite_note-78) A Hui from [Ningbo](/wiki/Ningbo "Ningbo") also told an Englishman that Hangzhou was the "stronghold" of Islam in Zhejiang province, containing multiple mosques, compared to his small congregation of around 30 families in Ningbo for his mosque.[[77]](#cite_note-79) Within the city of Hangzhou are two notable mosques: [New Hangzhou Great Mosque](/wiki/New_Hangzhou_Great_Mosque "New Hangzhou Great Mosque") and the [Phoenix Mosque](/wiki/Phoenix_Mosque "Phoenix Mosque"). As late as the latter part of the 16th and early 17th centuries, the city was an important center of [Chinese Jewry](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_China "History of the Jews in China"), and may have been the original home of the [Kaifeng Jewish community](/wiki/Kaifeng_Jews "Kaifeng Jews").[[78]](#cite_note-80) There was formerly a Jewish synagogue in Ningbo, as well as one in Hangzhou, but no traces of them are now discoverable, and the only Jews known to exist in China were in Kaifeng.[[79]](#cite_note-81) Two of the [Three Pillars of Chinese Catholicism](/wiki/Three_Pillars_of_Chinese_Catholicism "Three Pillars of Chinese Catholicism") were from Hangzhou. The [Immaculate Conception Cathedral of Hangzhou](/wiki/Immaculate_Conception_Cathedral_of_Hangzhou "Immaculate Conception Cathedral of Hangzhou") is one of the oldest [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic "Catholic") churches in China, dating back 400 years to the [Ming dynasty](/wiki/Ming_dynasty "Ming dynasty"). There was [persecution of Christians](/wiki/Persecution_of_Christians "Persecution of Christians") in the early 21st century in the city.[[80]](#cite_note-82)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/Chenghuangmiao_%28City_God_Pavilion%29%2C_Hangzhou%282568%29.jpg/250px-Chenghuangmiao_%28City_God_Pavilion%29%2C_Hangzhou%282568%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chenghuangmiao_(City_God_Pavilion),_Hangzhou(2568).jpg)

*Chenghuangmiao* located on Wushan, Hangzhou

There are many temples near the West Lake. [Lingyin Temple](/wiki/Lingyin_Temple "Lingyin Temple") was founded in the first year of Xianhe in the Eastern Jin Dynasty (AD 326). It has a history of about 1,700 years and is the earliest famous temple in Hangzhou. [Yuefei Temple](/wiki/Yuefei_Temple "Yuefei Temple"), a temple constructed during the [Song Dynasty](/wiki/Song_Dynasty "Song Dynasty") in 1221 to commemorate [Yue Fei](/wiki/Yue_Fei "Yue Fei"), is located near the [West Lake](/wiki/West_Lake "West Lake"). [Lingyin Temple](/wiki/Lingyin_Temple "Lingyin Temple") (Soul's Retreat), located about 2 km (1.2 mi) west of West Lake, is believed to be the oldest [Buddhist temple](/wiki/Buddhist_temple "Buddhist temple") in the city, which has gone through numerous destruction and reconstruction cycles. [Yue Fei Temple](/wiki/Yue_Fei_Temple "Yue Fei Temple"), on the northwest shore of West Lake, was originally constructed in 1221 in memory of General [Yue Fei](/wiki/Yue_Fei "Yue Fei"), who died due to political persecution. There is also the [Jingci Temple](/wiki/Jingci_Temple "Jingci Temple"), the [Baochu Pagoda](/wiki/Baochu_Pagoda "Baochu Pagoda"), and the [Leifeng Pagoda](/wiki/Leifeng_Pagoda "Leifeng Pagoda"). The [Qiantang River](/wiki/Qiantang_River "Qiantang River") is the largest river in Zhejiang Province, China. Every year during August 15 to August 18 of the lunar month in China, the Qiantang Tide occurs. It is called "the Biggest Tide in the World".

Other religious sites in Hangzhou include the [Liuhe Pagoda](/wiki/Liuhe_Pagoda "Liuhe Pagoda"), located on Yuelun Hill on the north bank of Qiantang River and the [Hupao Temple](/w/index.php?title=Hupao_Temple&action=edit&redlink=1 "Hupao Temple (page does not exist)") (虎跑寺).[[81]](#cite_note-83)

In 2019, Hangzhou established a pilot program artificial intelligence-based Internet Court to adjudicate disputes related to ecommerce and internet-related [intellectual property](/wiki/Intellectual_property_in_China "Intellectual property in China") claims.[[82]](#cite_note-:9222-84): 124 Parties appear before the court via videoconference and AI evaluates the evidence presented and applies relevant legal standards.[[82]](#cite_note-:9222-84): 124

## Administrative divisions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hangzhou&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Administrative divisions")]

Hangzhou is classified as a [sub-provincial city](/wiki/Sub-provincial_city "Sub-provincial city")[[12]](#cite_note-docin1-13) and forms the core of the Hangzhou metropolitan area,[[73]](#cite_note-ssdph.com.cn-75) the fourth-largest in China.[[83]](#cite_note-85) It is the capital and most populous city of [Zhejiang Province](/wiki/Zhejiang "Zhejiang") in [East China](/wiki/East_China "East China").[[9]](#cite_note-:04-10): 86 Hangzhou comprises 10 [districts](/wiki/District_(China) "District (China)"), 1 [county-level city](/wiki/County-level_city "County-level city"), and 2 [counties](/wiki/Counties_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "Counties of the People's Republic of China"). The ten urban districts occupy 8,292.31 km2 (3,201.68 sq mi) and have a population of 8,241,000, in which there are six central urban districts and four suburban districts. The central urban districts occupy 706.27 km2 (272.69 sq mi) and have a population of 3,780,000 and the suburban districts occupy 7,586.04 km2 (2,928.99 sq mi) and have a population of 4,461,000. The [West Lake Scenic Area](/wiki/Management_Committee_of_West_Lake_and_Xixi "Management Committee of West Lake and Xixi") holds the country-level administrative power, though not formally a county or district.[[84]](#cite_note-info-86)

In the early 90s, the urban districts of Hangzhou only comprised Shangcheng, Xiacheng, Gongshu, Jianggan. On December 11, 1996, [Binjiang District](/wiki/Binjiang_District "Binjiang District") was established. On March 12, 2001, Xiaoshan and Yuhang, formerly two [county-level cities](/wiki/County-level_city "County-level city") under the administration of Hangzhou [prefecture-level city](/wiki/Prefecture-level_city "Prefecture-level city"), were re-organized as two districts. On December 13, 2014, and in July 2017, Fuyang and Lin'an, formerly two [county-level cities](/wiki/County-level_city "County-level city") under the administration of Hangzhou [prefecture-level city](/wiki/Prefecture-level_city "Prefecture-level city"), were re-organized as two districts. On April 9, 2021, [Linping District](/wiki/Linping_District "Linping District") and [Qiantang District](/wiki/Qiantang_District "Qiantang District") was established.[[85]](#cite_note-87)[[86]](#cite_note-88)

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hangzhou&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Economy")]

Hangzhou's economy has rapidly developed since its opening up in 1992. It is an industrial city with many diverse sectors such as light industry, agriculture, and textiles. It is considered an important manufacturing base and logistics hub for coastal China.[[88]](#cite_note-hzindus.gov.cn-90) Additionally, the city is an [e-commerce](/wiki/E-commerce "E-commerce") and technology hub.[[89]](#cite_note-91) The 2001 GDP of Hangzhou was [RMB](/wiki/Renminbi "Renminbi") 156.8 billion, which ranked second among all of the provincial capitals after Guangzhou. The city has more than tripled its GDP since then, increasing from RMB 156.8 billion in 2001 to RMB 1.3509 trillion in 2018 and GDP per capita increasing from US$3,020 to $21,184.[[88]](#cite_note-hzindus.gov.cn-90)[[90]](#cite_note-92) As of 2019, the Hangzhou metropolitan area was estimated to produce a [gross metropolitan product](/wiki/Gross_metropolitan_product "Gross metropolitan product") ([nominal](/wiki/GDP_(nominal) "GDP (nominal)")) of 3.2 trillion yuan ($486.53 billion),[[91]](#cite_note-93) [making it larger than the economies](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(nominal) "List of countries by GDP (nominal)") of Argentina, with a GDP of $452 billion (the 26th biggest in the World) and Nigeria with a GDP of $448 billion (the largest in Africa).[[92]](#cite_note-94)

A study conducted by [PwC](/wiki/PricewaterhouseCoopers "PricewaterhouseCoopers") and China Development Research Foundation saw Hangzhou ranked first among "Chinese Cities of Opportunity".[[93]](#cite_note-95) Hangzhou is also considered a [World City](/wiki/Global_city "Global city") with a "Beta+" classification according to [GaWC](/wiki/Globalization_and_World_Cities_Research_Network "Globalization and World Cities Research Network").[[94]](#cite_note-96) Hangzhou ranked 89 in the [Global Financial Centres Index](/wiki/Global_Financial_Centres_Index "Global Financial Centres Index") in 2018.[[95]](#cite_note-GFCI-97) It was also ranked first in the China Emerging City Rankings of the [Economist Intelligence Unit](/wiki/Economist_Intelligence_Unit "Economist Intelligence Unit"), which assesses Chinese cities growth potential, in both 2021 and 2022.[[96]](#cite_note-98) Hangzhou ranks 11th in the world and 6th in China (after [Beijing](/wiki/Beijing "Beijing"), [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai "Shanghai"), [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong"), [Shenzhen](/wiki/Shenzhen "Shenzhen") and [Guangzhou](/wiki/Guangzhou "Guangzhou")) in terms of the number of billionaires according to the Hurun Global Rich List 2020.[[97]](#cite_note-:17-99) As of August 2023, Hangzhou has the tenth-most [Fortune Global 500](/wiki/Fortune_Global_500 "Fortune Global 500") headquarters of any city in the world and the fourth-most in China – after Beijing, Shanghai and Shenzhen – within its city limits.[[98]](#cite_note-:1-100)

Hangzhou has a [smart city](/wiki/Smart_city "Smart city") initiative and undertakes efforts to digitize the economy and build a cashless city.[[99]](#cite_note-:Liu-101): 122–123 [[100]](#cite_note-102)

### Industries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hangzhou&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Industries")]

Hangzhou is the headquarters of several technology companies including [Alibaba Group](/wiki/Alibaba_Group "Alibaba Group"), [NetEase](/wiki/NetEase "NetEase"),[[9]](#cite_note-:04-10): 90 [Ant Group](/wiki/Ant_Group "Ant Group"), [DeepSeek](/wiki/DeepSeek "DeepSeek"), [Geely](/wiki/Geely "Geely"), and [HikVision](/wiki/Hikvision "Hikvision"). In May 2024 Alibaba opened a new global headquarters that measured around one million sqm across seven buildings for 30,000 employees.[[101]](#cite_note-103) As a result of its internet industry, many programmers from other cities such as Shanghai or Beijing have come to Hangzhou.[[102]](#cite_note-104) The city has developed many new industries, including medicine, information technology, heavy equipment, automotive components, household electrical appliances, electronics, telecommunication, fine chemicals, chemical fibre and food processing.[[103]](#cite_note-105) The city describes its important industries as "1 + 6" industrial clusters, with the "1" referring to the digital economy and the "6" referring to cultural/creative economy, finance, tourism, fashion manufacturing, and high-end equipment manufacturing.[[9]](#cite_note-:04-10): 91 As of at least 2023, Hangzhou's economic growth has been led by the digital sector and the creative/cultural sectors.[[9]](#cite_note-:04-10): 91 Studies show that the total revenue of Alibaba Group has a positive effect on economic growth of the city but it is only a unidirectional casualty between revenue of the Group and the economic growth in Hangzhou. Specifically a 1% increase in total revenue of Alibaba Group can result in 0.272% in economic growth of the city in the long run.[[104]](#cite_note-106)

### Tourism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hangzhou&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Tourism")]

In March 2013, the Hangzhou Tourism Commission started an online campaign via Facebook, the 'Modern Marco Polo' campaign. Over the next year nearly 26,000 participants applied from around the globe, in the hopes of becoming Hangzhou's first foreign tourism ambassador. In a press conference in Hangzhou on 20 May 2014, [Liam Bates](/wiki/Liam_Bates "Liam Bates") was announced as the successful winner and won a $55,000 contract, being the first foreigner ever to be appointed by China's government in such an official role.[[105]](#cite_note-107)

### Development zones

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hangzhou&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Development zones")]

Hangzhou Economic and Technological Development Zone was established and approved as a national development zone by the [State Council](/wiki/State_Council_of_China "State Council of China") in 1993. It covers an area of 104.7 km2 (40.4 sq mi). Encouraged industries include electronic information, biological medicine, machinery and household appliances manufacturing, and food processing.[[106]](#cite_note-108) Hangzhou Export Processing Zone was established on April 27, 2000, upon approval of the State Council. It was one of the first zones and the only one in Zhejiang Province to be approved by the government. Its total planned area is 2.92 km2 (1.13 sq mi). It is located close to [Hangzhou Xiaoshan International Airport](/wiki/Hangzhou_Xiaoshan_International_Airport "Hangzhou Xiaoshan International Airport") and Hangzhou Port.[[107]](#cite_note-109)

Hangzhou Hi-Tech Industrial Development Zone was set up with approval from the State Council as a state-level high-tech Industrial Development Zone in March 1991. The HHTZ is composed of three parts, with the main regions being the Zhijiang Sci-Tech Industrial Park and Xiasha Sci-Tech Industrial Park. HHTZ has become one of the most influential high-tech innovation and high-tech industry bases in Zhejiang Province. As of 2013[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Hangzhou&action=edit), HHTZ hosts more than 1,100 software developers and [BPO](/wiki/Business_process_outsourcing "Business process outsourcing") enterprises. Major companies such as [Motorola](/wiki/Motorola "Motorola"), [Nokia](/wiki/Nokia "Nokia") and [Siemens](/wiki/Siemens "Siemens") have established R&D centers in the zone. In 2011, the GDP of the zone rose by 13.1 percent, amounting to RMB 41.63 billion. This accounted for 5.9 percent of Hangzhou's total GDP. The HHTZ positions itself as the "Silicon Valley" of China. The Alibaba Group is headquartered in the zone.[[108]](#cite_note-110)[[109]](#cite_note-111) In 2023 Alibaba sped up construction phase of its global headquarters in Hangzhou amid support from the local government after the company signed a fresh cooperation pact with local authorities.[[110]](#cite_note-112)

Although Hangzhou has been through many recent urban developments, it still retains its historical and cultural heritage and natural environment. Today, [tourism](/wiki/Tourism_in_China "Tourism in China") remains an important factor for Hangzhou's economy.[[111]](#cite_note-Hangzhou_Today:_Tourism-113) Hangzhou has [numerous skyscrapers](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Hangzhou "List of tallest buildings in Hangzhou"), making it the 19th [city in the world with the most skyscrapers](/wiki/List_of_cities_with_the_most_skyscrapers "List of cities with the most skyscrapers") as well as the 9th in China.[[112]](#cite_note-114)

### Parks and resorts

Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *Find sources:* ["Hangzhou"](https://www.google.com/search?as_eq=wikipedia&q=%22Hangzhou%22) – [news](https://www.google.com/search?tbm=nws&q=%22Hangzhou%22+-wikipedia&tbs=ar:1) **·** [newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Hangzhou%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Hangzhou%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Hangzhou%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Hangzhou%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(August 2019)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

One of Hangzhou's most popular attractions is [West Lake](/wiki/West_Lake "West Lake"), a [UNESCO World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site"). The West Lake Cultural Landscape spans approximately 3,323 ha (8,210 acres) and includes some of the city's most notable historic and scenic locations. Surrounding the lake are prominent cultural sites, historical pagodas, and natural features such as hills and tea fields, including [Phoenix Mountain](/wiki/Phoenix_Mountain_(Zhejiang) "Phoenix Mountain (Zhejiang)"). Two major [causeways](/wiki/Causeway "Causeway") cross the lake.[[111]](#cite_note-Hangzhou_Today:_Tourism-113)

To the west of the lake lies [Dreaming of the Tiger Spring](/wiki/Dreaming_of_the_Tiger_Spring "Dreaming of the Tiger Spring"), an area known for its natural spring and its proximity to the famed [longjing tea](/wiki/Longjing_tea "Longjing tea") plantations.[[113]](#cite_note-the_tea_fields_of_Longjing-115)

Sections of the [Grand Canal](/wiki/Grand_Canal_(China) "Grand Canal (China)") within Hangzhou—also designated a UNESCO World Heritage Site—were originally constructed in 610 AD. Key historical sites along the canal can be accessed via Hangzhou Metro [Line 5](/wiki/Line_5_(Hangzhou_Metro) "Line 5 (Hangzhou Metro)") at [The Grand Canal station](/wiki/The_Grand_Canal_station "The Grand Canal station") or [East Gongchen Bridge station](/wiki/East_Gongchen_Bridge_station "East Gongchen Bridge station").

The [West Lake Cultural Square](/wiki/West_Lake_Cultural_Square "West Lake Cultural Square"), located in the [Xiacheng District](/wiki/Xiacheng_District "Xiacheng District"), contains several notable institutions, including the [Zhejiang Natural History Museum](/wiki/Zhejiang_Natural_History_Museum "Zhejiang Natural History Museum"), the Zhejiang Museum of Science and Technology, and the Zhejiang Global Center, one of the tallest buildings in the downtown area at approximately 160 m (520 ft).

The [Xixi National Wetland Park](/wiki/Xixi_National_Wetland_Park "Xixi National Wetland Park") was established to preserve the region's wetland ecosystem and encompasses about 10 km2 (3.9 sq mi). Restoration projects have included reed beds and fish ponds, and the park is now home to diverse bird species. The area also contains a temple and several historically significant rural dwellings.

[Qiandao Lake](/wiki/Qiandao_Lake "Qiandao Lake"), a man-made reservoir located in [Chun'an County](/wiki/Chun%27an_County "Chun'an County") under Hangzhou's jurisdiction, contains the largest number of islands of any lake in China. These islands vary widely in size and form, each with distinctive scenery. The [Hangzhou Botanical Garden](/wiki/Hangzhou_Botanical_Garden "Hangzhou Botanical Garden") and the [Hangzhou Zoo](/wiki/Hangzhou_Zoo "Hangzhou Zoo") are both situated in the [Xihu District](/wiki/Xihu,_Hangzhou "Xihu, Hangzhou").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/Relics_Unearthed_from_Leifeng_Pagoda_Site_03_2017-12.jpg/250px-Relics_Unearthed_from_Leifeng_Pagoda_Site_03_2017-12.jpg)](/wiki/File:Relics_Unearthed_from_Leifeng_Pagoda_Site_03_2017-12.jpg)

Gilt silver [Hōkyōintō](/wiki/H%C5%8Dky%C5%8Dint%C5%8D "Hōkyōintō") unearthed from [Leifeng Pagoda](/wiki/Leifeng_Pagoda "Leifeng Pagoda") Site, [Zhejiang Provincial Museum](/wiki/Zhejiang_Provincial_Museum "Zhejiang Provincial Museum")

The native residents of Hangzhou, including those of [Zhejiang](/wiki/Zhejiang "Zhejiang") and southern [Jiangsu](/wiki/Jiangsu "Jiangsu"), speak the [Hangzhou dialect](/wiki/Hangzhou_dialect "Hangzhou dialect"), a [Wu](/wiki/Wu_Chinese "Wu Chinese") dialect unique to the area. Hangzhou's dialect differs from those of regions in southern Zhejiang and southern [Jiangsu](/wiki/Jiangsu "Jiangsu"). As the official language defined by China's central government, [Mandarin](/wiki/Standard_Chinese "Standard Chinese") is the dominant spoken language, though it is mutually unintelligible with the Hangzhou dialect. The Hangzhou dialect has an estimated total of 1.2 to 1.5 million speakers.

There are several museums located in Hangzhou including [China National Silk Museum](/wiki/China_National_Silk_Museum "China National Silk Museum"), the largest silk museum in the world, [China National Tea Museum](/wiki/China_National_Tea_Museum "China National Tea Museum") (中国茶叶博物馆), and [Zhejiang Provincial Museum](/wiki/Zhejiang_Provincial_Museum "Zhejiang Provincial Museum"), which has a collection of integrated human studies, exhibition and research with over 100,000 collected cultural relics.

Many theaters in Hangzhou host opera shows such as [Yue opera](/wiki/Yue_opera "Yue opera"). There are several big shows themed with the history and culture of Hangzhou like [Impression West Lake](https://web.archive.org/web/20140605035555/http://www.hzyxxh.com/en/) and the Romance of Song Dynasty. The landscapes in Hangzhou bridges stories of celebrities in Chinese history and feelings of ordinary people visiting Hangzhou with joy and enthusiasm.[[114]](#cite_note-116)

Hangzhou is home to the [China Academy of Art](/wiki/China_Academy_of_Art "China Academy of Art") and prominent painters such as [Lin Fengmian](/wiki/Lin_Fengmian "Lin Fengmian") and [Fang Ganmin](/wiki/Fang_Ganmin "Fang Ganmin").

The local government of Hangzhou heavily invests in promoting tourism and the arts, with emphasis placed upon [silk](/wiki/Silk "Silk") production, [umbrellas](/wiki/Umbrella "Umbrella"), and Chinese hand-held folding [fans](/wiki/Fan_(implement) "Fan (implement)").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Cuisine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hangzhou&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Cuisine")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/Xi_Hu_Longjing_Tea_01.jpg/250px-Xi_Hu_Longjing_Tea_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Xi_Hu_Longjing_Tea_01.jpg)

Xihu Longjing (西湖龙井), Longjing tea planted near the West Lake

Hangzhou's local cuisine is often considered to be representative of [Zhejiang provincial cuisine](/wiki/Zhejiang_cuisine "Zhejiang cuisine"), one of [China's eight fundamental cuisines](/wiki/Chinese_cuisine "Chinese cuisine"). The locally accepted consensus among Hangzhou's natives defines dishes prepared in this style to be "fresh, tender, soft, and smooth, with a mellow fragrance".[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Generally, Hangzhou's cuisines tend to be sweeter rather than savoury. The local people enjoy a light diet incorporating river fishes from the Yangtze River. There are historical stories revolving around the origins of local dishes.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/be/Fish_on_a_plate_in_a_restaurant_in_Hangzhou%2C_China_-_December_2011.jpg/250px-Fish_on_a_plate_in_a_restaurant_in_Hangzhou%2C_China_-_December_2011.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fish_on_a_plate_in_a_restaurant_in_Hangzhou,_China_-_December_2011.jpg)

West Lake Fish in Vinegar Gravy

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/BCfood12.JPG/250px-BCfood12.JPG)](/wiki/File:BCfood12.JPG)

**Dongpo pork**

Dishes such as Pian Er Chuan Noodles (片儿川), [West Lake Vinegar Fish](/wiki/West_Lake_Fish_in_Vinegar_Gravy "West Lake Fish in Vinegar Gravy") (西湖醋鱼), [Dongpo Pork](/wiki/Dongpo_Pork "Dongpo Pork") (东坡肉), Longjing Shrimp (龙井虾仁), [Beggar's Chicken](/wiki/Beggar%27s_Chicken "Beggar's Chicken") (叫化鸡), Steamed Rice and Pork Wrapped by Lotus Leaves(荷叶粉蒸肉), Braised Bamboo Shoots (油焖笋), Lotus Root Pudding (藕粉) and Sister Song's Fish Soup (宋嫂鱼羹) are some of the better-known examples of Hangzhou's regional cuisine.

Hangzhou is known for originating [Longjing tea](/wiki/Longjing_tea "Longjing tea"), a notable variety of [green tea](/wiki/Green_tea "Green tea").[[115]](#cite_note-117) It is the most famous green tea and the most famous tea in China.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The tea planted by the [West Lake](/wiki/West_Lake "West Lake") is considered the best Longjing tea.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Tea is an important part of Hangzhou's economy and culture.

### Proverbs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hangzhou&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Proverbs")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This section **may contain [original research](/wiki/Wikipedia:No_original_research "Wikipedia:No original research")**. Please [improve it](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Hangzhou&action=edit) by [verifying](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability") the claims made and adding [inline citations](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#Inline_citations "Wikipedia:Citing sources"). Statements consisting only of original research should be removed. *(January 2025)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

An ancient Chinese proverb about Hangzhou and [Suzhou](/wiki/Suzhou "Suzhou") is:

> There is Heaven above, and Suzhou and Hangzhou below. (上有天堂，下有苏杭)

This phrase has a similar meaning to the English phrases "Heaven on Earth". [Marco Polo](/wiki/Marco_Polo "Marco Polo") in his accounts described Suzhou as "the city of the earth" while Hangzhou is "the city of heaven".[[116]](#cite_note-118) The city presented itself as "Paradise on Earth" during the [G20 summit](/wiki/2016_G20_Hangzhou_summit "2016 G20 Hangzhou summit") held in the city in 2016.[[117]](#cite_note-119)

Another saying about Hangzhou is:

> Be born in [Suzhou](/wiki/Suzhou,_Jiangsu "Suzhou, Jiangsu"), live in Hangzhou, eat in [Guangzhou](/wiki/Guangzhou "Guangzhou"), die in [Liuzhou](/wiki/Liuzhou "Liuzhou"). (生在苏州，活在杭州，吃在广州，死在柳州)

### Ancient book restoration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hangzhou&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Ancient book restoration")]

The Hangzhou Ancient Book Restoration and Display Center, affiliated with the [Zhejiang Library](/wiki/Zhejiang_Library "Zhejiang Library"), offers regular public demonstrations of traditional paper restoration and binding techniques used to preserve ancient Chinese texts. This center plays a key role in the conservation of [Jiangnan](/wiki/Jiangnan "Jiangnan") cultural documents.[[118]](#cite_note-120)

## Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hangzhou&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Transportation")]

### Public

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hangzhou&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Public")]

Hangzhou has a bus network consisting of a fleet of diesel, hybrid and electric buses, as well as [trolleybuses](/wiki/Trolleybus "Trolleybus"). Hangzhou was once known for its extensive [bus rapid transit](/wiki/Bus_rapid_transit "Bus rapid transit") network expanding from downtown to many suburban areas through dedicated bus lanes on some of the busiest streets in the city. However, as of mid-2021, all but one BRT routes and feeding routes had closed or been transformed to regular routes. Only route B1 is still in operation.

[Taxis](/wiki/Taxicab "Taxicab") are also popular in the city, with the newest line of [Hyundai Sonatas](/wiki/Hyundai_Sonata "Hyundai Sonata") and [Volkswagen Passats](/wiki/Volkswagen_Passat "Volkswagen Passat"), and tight regulations. In early 2011, 30 electric taxis were deployed in Hangzhou; 15 were [Zotye](/wiki/Zotye "Zotye") Langyues and the other 15 were [Haima](/wiki/Haima_(car) "Haima (car)") Freemas. In April, however, one Zoyte Langyue caught fire, and all of the electric taxis were taken off the roads later that day. The city still intends to have a fleet of 200 electric taxis by the end of 2011.[[119]](#cite_note-121) In 2014, a large number of new electric taxis produced by Xihu-BYD (Xihu (westlake) is a local company which produced televisions in the past) were deployed.

Central (to the east of the city centre, taking the place of the former east station), north, south, and west long-distance coach stations offer frequent coach service to nearby cities/towns within Zhejiang province, as well as surrounding provinces.

[Hangzhou Metro](/wiki/Hangzhou_Metro "Hangzhou Metro") has a network of 323 km as of mid-2021, not including the [Hangzhou-Haining Intercity Railway](/wiki/Hangzhou%E2%80%93Haining_intercity_railway "Hangzhou–Haining intercity railway") which has a length of 46 km. Major expansion plans continue. It is the 17th city in China to have a rapid rail transit system. In 2018, the [State Council](/wiki/State_Council_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "State Council of the People's Republic of China") approved the planning for 15 metro lines, including extensions to the three existing lines, scheduled to open in time for the [2022 Asian Games](/wiki/2022_Asian_Games "2022 Asian Games").[[120]](#cite_note-122) By then the Hangzhou Metro system is projected have a network of 617 km (383 mi).[[121]](#cite_note-123)

The construction of the Metro started in March 2006, and Line 1 opened on November 24, 2012.[[122]](#cite_note-本网快讯：杭州地铁时代即将来临_1号线24日开通-124) Line 1 connects city centre with suburbs. It run from Xianghu to Wenze Road with a branch to Lingping, which would later become part of Line 9. By June 2015, the southeast section of Line 2 (starts in Xiaoshan District, ends to the south of the city centre) and a short part of Line 4 (fewer than 10 stations, connecting Line 1 and Line 2) were completed. The system is expected to have 15 lines upon completion; most lines are still under construction. The extensions of Line 2 (city centre and northwest Hangzhou) and Line 4 (east of [Binjiang District](/wiki/Binjiang_District "Binjiang District")) opened in 2018. Line 5/6/7/8 opened their first parts in 2019 and 2020.

### Cycle hire

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hangzhou&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Cycle hire")]

[Bicycles](/wiki/Bicycle "Bicycle") and [electric scooters](/wiki/Motorized_bicycle "Motorized bicycle") are very popular, and major streets have dedicated bike lanes throughout the city. Hangzhou has an extensive [public bike rental system](/wiki/Bicycle_sharing_system "Bicycle sharing system") called the [Hangzhou Public Bicycle](/wiki/Hangzhou_Public_Bicycle "Hangzhou Public Bicycle") system. There is a dock-and-station system like those of Paris or London and users can hire bicycles with IC card or mobile phone application. Journeys within 60 minutes are free of charge.

### Railways

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hangzhou&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Railways")]

Hangzhou sits on the intersecting point of some of the busiest rail corridors in China. The city's main station is [Hangzhou East station](/wiki/Hangzhou_East_railway_station "Hangzhou East railway station") (colloquially "East Station" 东站). It is one of the biggest rail traffic hubs in China, consisting of 15 platforms that house the High Speed services to Shanghai, Nanjing, Changsha, Ningbo, and beyond. The metro station beneath the rail complex building is a stop along the Hangzhou Metro Line 1 and Line 4. There are frequent departures for [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai "Shanghai") with approximately 20-minute headways from 6:00 to 21:00. Non-stop CRH high-speed service between Hangzhou and Shanghai takes 50 minutes and leaves every hour (excluding a few early morning/late night departures) from both directions. Other [CRH](/wiki/China_Railway_High-speed "China Railway High-speed") high-speed trains that stop at one or more stations along the route complete the trip in 59 to 75 minutes. Most other major cities in China can also be reached by direct train service from Hangzhou. The [Hangzhou railway station](/wiki/Hangzhou_railway_station "Hangzhou railway station") (colloquially the "City Station" Chinese: 城站) was closed for renovation in mid 2013 but has recently opened again.

A second high-speed rail channel through Hangzhou is operational along with another major station, [Hangzhou West](/wiki/Hangzhou_West_railway_station "Hangzhou West railway station"), opened on September 22, 2022.[[123]](#cite_note-125)

Direct trains link Hangzhou with more than 50 main cities, including 12 daily services to [Beijing](/wiki/Beijing "Beijing") and more than 100 daily services to [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai "Shanghai"); they reach as far as [Ürümqi](/wiki/%C3%9Cr%C3%BCmqi "Ürümqi"). The [China Railway High-Speed](/wiki/China_Railway_High-Speed "China Railway High-Speed") service inaugurated on October 26, 2010. The service is operated by the CRH 380A(L), CRH 380B(L) and CRH380CL train sets which travel at a maximum speed of 350 km/h (220 mph), shortening the duration of the 202 km (126 mi) trip to only 45 minutes.[[124]](#cite_note-126)

### Air and sea

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hangzhou&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Air and sea")]

Hangzhou is served by the [Hangzhou Xiaoshan International Airport](/wiki/Hangzhou_Xiaoshan_International_Airport "Hangzhou Xiaoshan International Airport"), which provides direct service to many international destinations such as [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand"), [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan"), [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea "South Korea"), [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia"), [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam"), [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore"), [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands"),[[125]](#cite_note-127) [Qatar](/wiki/Qatar "Qatar"), [Portugal](/wiki/Portugal "Portugal") and the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), as well as regional routes to [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong"), [Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan"), and [Macau](/wiki/Macau "Macau"). It has an extensive domestic route network within the PRC and is consistently ranked top 10 in passenger traffic among Chinese airports.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The Port of Hangzhou is a small river port with a cargo throughput that exceeds 100 million tons annually.[[126]](#cite_note-128)

## Education and research

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hangzhou&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Education and research")]

See also: [List of universities in China](/wiki/List_of_universities_in_China "List of universities in China") and [Double First-Class Construction](/wiki/Double_First-Class_Construction "Double First-Class Construction")

Hangzhou is a major city for education and scientific research in China and Asia, ranking 6th in the Asia-Oceania region and 10th globally by the [Nature Index](/wiki/Nature_Index "Nature Index") as of 2025.[[127]](#cite_note-129) Hangzhou hosts many universities, most notably [Zhejiang University](/wiki/Zhejiang_University "Zhejiang University"), one of the world's top 50th comprehensive public research universities[[128]](#cite_note-130)[[129]](#cite_note-131)[[130]](#cite_note-132)[[131]](#cite_note-133) and a member of the [C9 League](/wiki/C9_League "C9 League"), an alliance of elite Chinese universities offering comprehensive and leading education.[[132]](#cite_note-134)

Hangzhou has a large student population, with college towns such as Xiasha, located near the east end of the city, and Xiaoheshan, located near the west end of the city. Universities in Hangzhou include [China Academy of Art](/wiki/China_Academy_of_Art "China Academy of Art"), [China Jiliang University](/wiki/China_Jiliang_University "China Jiliang University"), Provincial key public high schools in Hangzhou include [Hangzhou No. 2 High School](/wiki/Hangzhou_No._2_High_School "Hangzhou No. 2 High School"), [Hangzhou Xuejun High School](/wiki/Hangzhou_Xuejun_High_School "Hangzhou Xuejun High School"), [Hangzhou High School](/wiki/Hangzhou_High_School "Hangzhou High School"), [Hangzhou No. 14 High School](/wiki/Hangzhou_No._14_High_School "Hangzhou No. 14 High School"), [Hangzhou No. 4 High School](/wiki/Hangzhou_No._4_High_School "Hangzhou No. 4 High School"), [High School Attached to Zhejiang University](/wiki/High_School_Attached_to_Zhejiang_University "High School Attached to Zhejiang University"), [The Affiliated High School to Hangzhou Normal University](/w/index.php?title=The_Affiliated_High_School_to_Hangzhou_Normal_University&action=edit&redlink=1 "The Affiliated High School to Hangzhou Normal University (page does not exist)"), and [Hangzhou Foreign Language School](/wiki/Hangzhou_Foreign_Language_School "Hangzhou Foreign Language School").

Private high schools in Hangzhou include [Dingwen Academy Hangzhou](/wiki/Dingwen_Academy_Hangzhou "Dingwen Academy Hangzhou"), [Hangzhou Green Town Yuhua School](/w/index.php?title=Hangzhou_Green_Town_Yuhua_School&action=edit&redlink=1 "Hangzhou Green Town Yuhua School (page does not exist)"), [Hangzhou Chinese International School](/w/index.php?title=Hangzhou_Chinese_International_School&action=edit&redlink=1 "Hangzhou Chinese International School (page does not exist)"), [Hangzhou International School](/wiki/Hangzhou_International_School "Hangzhou International School") and [Hangzhou Japanese School](/w/index.php?title=Hangzhou_Japanese_School&action=edit&redlink=1 "Hangzhou Japanese School (page does not exist)") (杭州日本人学校) ([nihonjin gakkō](/wiki/Nihonjin_gakk%C5%8D "Nihonjin gakkō")).[[134]](#cite_note-136)

1. **[^](#cite_ref-9)** [/hɑːŋˈdʒoʊ/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*hahng-JOH*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7) or [/hæŋˈdʒoʊ/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*hang-JOH*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key");[[8]](#cite_note-8) [Chinese](/wiki/Simplified_Chinese_characters "Simplified Chinese characters"): 杭州, Wu Chinese pronunciation: [[ɦɑ̃.tse]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Wu_Chinese "Help:IPA/Wu Chinese"), [Standard Mandarin](/wiki/Standard_Chinese "Standard Chinese") pronunciation: [[xǎŋ.ʈʂóʊ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Mandarin "Help:IPA/Mandarin") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Hang2zhou1.ogg "File:Hang2zhou1.ogg"); formerly romanized as **Hangchow**

1. **[^](#cite_ref-48)** For a discussion of the many sources and variant spellings of the names, see [Moule](/wiki/Arthur_Christopher_Moule "Arthur Christopher Moule").[[45]](#cite_note-46) The ultimate Chinese source of these names has been variously given as *Jīngshī* ([京師](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E4%BA%AC%E5%B8%AB "wikt:京師"), "the Capital"); *Xingzai*, an abbreviated form of *Xíngzàisuǒ* ([行在](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E8%A1%8C%E5%9C%A8 "wikt:行在")[所](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E6%89%80 "wikt:所"), "the Place of Temporary Residence"), which had formerly been a byname for the Song capital from the hope that the court would eventually return north to [Kaifeng](/wiki/Kaifeng "Kaifeng"); and *Hangtsei*, the [Hangzhounese](/wiki/Hangzhounese "Hangzhounese") pronunciation of the town's name.[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoule1957[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidUYIoAAAAQBAJpgPA4_4]-47)

[Wikisource](/wiki/Wikisource "Wikisource") has the text of the 1905 *[New International Encyclopedia](/wiki/New_International_Encyclopedia "New International Encyclopedia")* article "**[Hang-chow](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/The_New_International_Encyclop%C3%A6dia/Hang-chow "s:The New International Encyclopædia/Hang-chow")**".
